extern type Number;
cap Add { fn add(a: Self, b: Self): Self }
extern fn js_add(a: Number, b: Number): Number;
impl Number: Add { fn add(a: Self, b: Self): Self = resume(js_add(a, b)) }

fn apply_thunk(job: thunk Number): Number = Add.add(force job, 1)
fn use_thunk(): Number = apply_thunk(thunk 10)
fn main(): Number = Add.add(use_thunk(), use_thunk())

===EXPECT===
# Indirect-call-adjacent fn: `apply_thunk` takes a `thunk Number` parameter
# and does `force job` inside. The dep_free analyzer's call-graph classifier
# records only the OUTER Apply's head; `force job` alone (used as an argument)
# never becomes a recorded `CallTarget::Indirect` because it isn't the head
# of an Apply. So `apply_thunk` is classified dep-free and gets inlined at
# its single call site in `use_thunk`.
#
# v1 limitation (documented): the cloning heuristic operates on LIR structure
# but doesn't update upstream fn signatures. `use_thunk`'s cap signature was
# already committed during typecheck (`/ { Add }`) before LTO. Post-LTO, its
# body no longer performs Add, yet it's still emitted in CPS form with unused
# `__caps`/`__k` plumbing — a visible perf leak.
#
# The "strong" indirect-call block (from dep_free's unit test `fn apply(f:
# thunk Number, x: Number): Number { f(x) }`) requires calling `f(x)`, which
# the typechecker rejects because `thunk Number` isn't function-typed. So the
# `CallTarget::Indirect → Blocked` path isn't reachable from surface syntax
# today; the unit test exercises it via direct LIR construction.
#
# What this fixture pins down:
#   - `apply_thunk` IS inlined (body has `const a = job(); ...; js_add(a,b)`).
#   - `use_thunk` is NOT cloned as `use_thunk__lto` — no clone emitted.
#   - `use_thunk` retains unused `__caps` plumbing (v1 leak).
function use_thunk(__caps, __k)
__lto_job
js_add(__lto_
return use_thunk(__caps,
!use_thunk__lto
!apply_thunk__lto
!function apply_thunk(__caps
